programs.name: NC Medicaid Long-Term Care
programs._raw:
{
  "name": "NC Medicaid Long-Term Care",
  "eligibility": {
    "age": 65,
    "income_limits": "Income must be less than the Medicaid-approved cost of care in the facility, estimated at $8,004–$11,093/month (2025) or $7,898.40–$11,217.90/month (2026); varies by facility/county cost of care, not household size. For single medically needy applicants, Medicaid covers costs exceeding countable monthly income after deductions (e.g., $70/month personal needs allowance, health premiums, spousal allowances, home maintenance if return expected within 6 months).[1][4][5]",
    "asset_limits": "Single applicant: ≤$2,000 countable assets. Married both applying: ≤$3,000. Exempt: primary home (equity ≤$730,000 if intent to return or spouse/child under 21/disabled lives there); non-countable income deductions apply.[1][4][8][10]",
    "other_requirements": [
      "NC resident and US citizen/eligible immigrant.[1][6]",
      "Nursing Facility Level of Care (NFLOC): need care for 30+ consecutive days; assistance with ≥3 ADLs (mobility, bathing, dressing, eating, toileting) or extensive help with ≥2 ADLs; assessment of ADLs/IADLs and cognitive/behavioral issues.[1][2][4]",
      "Blind or disabled if under 65.[1][2]"
    ]
  },
  "benefits": {
    "type": "service",
    "value": "Nursing home care, assisted living, adult foster care (family care homes), home/community-based services (HCBS) waivers (e.g., personal care, adult day care, home modifications), non-medical supports for frail seniors; most income paid as patient liability to facility, Medicaid covers remainder.[4][5][9]",
    "varies_by": "priority_tier"
  },
  "application": {
    "methods": [
      "Online: NC Medicaid eligibility portal (implied via ncdhhs.gov).[6]",
      "Phone: Local Department of Social Services (DSS) offices (no statewide number specified; contact county DSS).[5]",
      "In-person/mail: County DSS offices.[5]"
    ],
    "processing_time": "Not specified in sources.",
    "waitlist": "Waitlists exist for HCBS waivers (e.g., CAP/DA); nursing home is entitlement if eligible.[2][3]",
    "forms": ["No specific form names/numbers provided; apply via county DSS or online Medicaid application.[6]"],
    "documents": ["Proof of residency, citizenship/immigration status, SSN, income, assets, medical/functional assessment records (ADLs)."][1][2][6]
  },
  "geography": {
    "statewide": true,
    "restrictions": "Financial thresholds vary by county/facility cost of care; PACE restricted to service areas.[3]",
    "regional_variations": "Income limits tied to local nursing home costs; different providers/wait times by county/region; apply via local DSS.[2][5]",
    "offices_or_providers": ["County Department of Social Services (DSS) offices statewide; PACE organizations in specific areas."][3][5]
  },
  "official_source": "https://medicaid.ncdhhs.gov/providers/programs-and-services/long-term-care",
  "gotchas": [
    "Income limit is facility-specific cost of care (varies by county), not fixed amount; most income becomes 'patient liability'.[1][2][5]",
    "HCBS waivers have waitlists, unlike nursing home entitlement.[2][3]",
    "Spousal rules complex for married couples (community spouse protections).[1][8]",
    "Must meet NFLOC; Regular Medicaid ABD covers basic care but limited LTC without full NFLOC.[2][4]",
    "Home equity limit $730,000; planning required to avoid penalties.[1]"
  ],
  "data_shape_notes": "Multiple programs (Nursing Home Medicaid, HCBS Waivers, ABD Medicaid) with shared financial rules but varying functional thresholds and waitlists; income/assets vary by marital status, county care costs, and program type."
}

programs.name: NC Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) Waivers

eligibility.age: Varies by waiver: CAP/DA (18+), CAP/C (0-20), TBI (18+), Innovations (all ages with I/DD)[1][2][5][8]
eligibility.income_limits: Must qualify for NC Medicaid; typically ~$1,255/month for individual (2025, subject to change); 2026 nursing home level est. $7,898-$11,218/month but waivers follow Medicaid standard ~$2,901/month individual; waiver slots use applicant income only, not family[1][2][3][4]
eligibility.asset_limits: $2,000 countable resources for individual; exempt: primary home (equity limit $752,000 in 2026 if no qualifying resident), car, personal belongings; most bank accounts, retirement, cash count[1][3][4]
other_requirements: Nursing Facility Level of Care (NFLOC) or equivalent institutional level required across waivers[1][3][4][5][8]
Medically fragile/complex or at risk of institutionalization; functional needs assessment (ADLs, mobility, health risks)[1][2]
Able to remain safely at home with services[1]
Specific: CAP/DA (disabled adults 18-64 or 65+ physical), CAP/C (medically fragile children), TBI (traumatic brain injury 18+), Innovations (I/DD or related conditions)[2][5][6][7][8]

benefits.value: Specific services vary by waiver; e.g., CAP/DA: personal assistance, adult day health, respite, home modifications, non-medical transport, nutritional services, PERS; CAP/C: in-home aide, respite, assistive tech, vehicle mods, nurse aide; TBI: day supports, therapies, home mods, supported living; Innovations: wide range for I/DD; no fixed $ or hours stated, cost-neutral to institutional care[1][2][4][5]

methods: CAP/DA: Confirm Medicaid via NC ePASS or local DSS, then request assessment[1]
CAP/C: Local Case Management Agency (directory: CAP-C Case Management Agency Directory) or Acentra Health[2]
Innovations: Local LME/MCO (NC DHHS LME/MCO Directory)[2]
General: Local DSS for Medicaid, then waiver-specific agency[1][2]
application.processing_time: Not specified; includes assessment after Medicaid approval[1]
application.waitlist: Yes for some (e.g., Innovations 'Registry of Unmet Needs'); others limited slots, not entitlement[2][3][4]
forms: NC ePASS for Medicaid; no specific waiver form named[1]
documents: Income/asset proof, medical records for NFLOC assessment, ID; consult local DSS[1][3]

geography.restrictions: Available in all 100 NC counties (e.g., TBI, Innovations)[2]
geography.regional_variations: Administered via local DSS, Case Management Agencies, LME/MCOs; wait times/providers may vary by region[2]
offices_or_providers: Local DSS, CAP/C Case Management Agencies, LME/MCO Directory[1][2]

gotchas: Multiple waivers exist (not one program): CAP/DA for disabled adults, CAP/C children, TBI, Innovations I/DD—match to needs[2][5][7][8]
Not entitlement: limited slots, waitlists common[3][4]
Must qualify for Medicaid first, but waiver uses applicant-only income[1][2]
NFLOC required, assessed via functional needs—not just age/disability[1][3]
Home equity limits apply ($752K 2026)[3]
Spend-down/Miller Trusts possible if over limits[1]
programs.data_shape_notes: Multiple distinct waivers (CAP/DA, CAP/C, TBI, Innovations) with target populations, all statewide but local admin; benefits/service arrays waiver-specific; waitlists and NFLOC universal; applicant-only income for Medicaid qual

programs.name: NC Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)

eligibility.income_limits: No income limit for eligibility. However, if you qualify for Medicaid long-term care, most states (including NC) cap income at 300% of the Federal Benefit Rate ($2,901/month as of 2025)[2]. If your income exceeds this, you may still enroll by paying a monthly Patient Monthly Liability determined by Medicaid based on your assets and income[3]. If you have only Medicare (no Medicaid), you pay a set monthly fee[3]. If you have neither Medicare nor Medicaid, you can private pay at approximately $4,000–$5,000/month[2].
eligibility.asset_limits: For Medicaid eligibility: $2,000 or less in countable assets (excluding primary home)[2]. The primary residence is exempt from asset calculations[2].
other_requirements: Must be certified by NC Medicaid as needing nursing facility level of care (extensive assistance with Activities of Daily Living: bathing, grooming, toileting, walking, transferring, eating)[2][4]
Must reside in the service area of a PACE organization[1][6]
Must be able to live safely in the community with PACE support at time of enrollment[1][3]
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage (Part C) plan, Medicare prepayment plan, or Medicare prescription drug plan[1]
Cannot be enrolled in hospice services or certain other programs[1]
Must be a U.S. citizen or legal resident for 5 years prior to application (if Medicare-eligible)[2]

benefits.value: Comprehensive managed care including: primary care, specialist care, hospital care, prescription drugs, therapy services, social services, transportation, and other services needed to support community living. Participants receive no deductibles, co-pays, or co-insurance for approved services[3]. PACE becomes both insurer and provider[8].
benefits.varies_by: funding_source — Medicare covers Medicare-eligible services; Medicaid covers Medicaid-eligible services; private pay covers all services at negotiated rates. Specific service mix is individualized based on care plan[3].

methods: Phone: Contact your local PACE Center directly[6]. NC PACE Association phone: 919-855-4340[4]
In-person: Visit your local PACE Center[6]
Self-referral or referral by family member, physician, or provider[6]
Online: Visit NC PACE Association website to locate local PACE centers[6][8]
application.documents: Not specified in available sources. Contact your local PACE Center for specific documentation requirements.

geography.restrictions: PACE is only available in certain areas of North Carolina where PACE organizations operate[7]. Participants must reside within the defined service area of a specific PACE organization[1][3][6].
geography.regional_variations: Different PACE organizations serve different regions. For example, Carolina SeniorCare serves Davidson, Rowan, Davie, and Iredell Counties[3]. Each PACE organization may have different service areas, staffing, and specific offerings.
offices_or_providers: Carolina SeniorCare (serves Davidson, Rowan, Davie, Iredell Counties)[3]
Other NC PACE centers (specific list available at NC PACE Association website)[6][8]

gotchas: PACE is NOT available statewide — you must live in a service area where a PACE organization operates[7]. Check availability before assuming eligibility[8].
You cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage (Part C) — if you have a Medicare Advantage plan, you must disenroll before joining PACE[1].
Nursing home level of care certification is required and is determined by the state, not by the program — this is a gatekeeping requirement[1][4].
If you exceed Medicaid income limits, you don't lose eligibility; instead, you pay a Patient Monthly Liability (out-of-pocket cost) determined by Medicaid[3].
Private pay costs are substantial (~$4,000–$5,000/month) if you have neither Medicare nor Medicaid[2].
All services except emergencies must be approved in advance by your PACE organization — using out-of-network providers without approval may result in full cost to you[3].
You can disenroll at any time, but PACE becomes your sole insurer and provider — you won't have separate health insurance coverage[3][8].
The average PACE participant is 76 years old with multiple complex medical conditions — this is designed for frail seniors, not those with minimal care needs[1].
programs.data_shape_notes: NC PACE is a regionally fragmented program with multiple independent PACE organizations, each serving specific counties. Eligibility is standardized statewide (age 55+, nursing home level of care, service area residency), but availability, service offerings, and costs vary by provider. Income limits apply only if seeking Medicaid coverage; otherwise, no income test exists. Asset limits ($2,000) apply only for Medicaid eligibility. The program is voluntary enrollment with no waitlist information publicly available. Processing timelines and required documentation are not standardized across providers.

## Key Takeaways for Families

**Before you start:** Confirm that a PACE organization operates in your county[8]. NC PACE is not available everywhere.

**The core eligibility test:** Your loved one must be 55+, need nursing home-level care (certified by NC Medicaid), live in a PACE service area, and be able to live safely at home with support[4].

**The financial picture:** If your loved one qualifies for Medicaid, costs are minimal or zero[3]. If they have Medicare only, there's a set monthly fee[3]. If they have neither, expect $4,000–$5,000/month[2]. Income doesn't disqualify you — it determines your out-of-pocket cost[8].

**The application process:** Call your local PACE Center or the NC PACE Association (919-855-4340)[4] to start. You can refer yourself, or a family member/doctor can refer[6]. Specific forms and documentation requirements vary by provider — ask when you call.

**The critical gotcha:** PACE replaces your health insurance entirely[8]. You cannot stay in a Medicare Advantage plan[1]. All services must be pre-approved[3].

programs.name: NC Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)

eligibility.income_limits: Income limits for 2026 vary by program and household size, expressed as percentages of the Federal Poverty Level (FPL) with a $20 general income disregard. Use the most recent figures from authoritative sources: QMB (≤100% FPL): Individual $1,325-$1,350/mo, Couple $1,783-$1,824/mo; SLMB (100-120% FPL): Individual $1,585-$1,616/mo, Couple $2,135-$2,184/mo; QI (120-135% FPL): Individual $1,816/mo, Couple $2,455/mo. Older sources list lower amounts (e.g., QMB Individual $1,235), so verify current FPL-adjusted limits with NC DHHS.[1][2][4][6]
eligibility.asset_limits: Resources ≤$9,950 (individual) or $14,910 (couple) across QMB, SLMB, QI. Countable resources include bank accounts, stocks; exempt: home, one car, burial plots, life insurance (up to certain values), personal belongings. Some sources note slightly lower prior-year limits ($9,290/$14,810 or $9,090/$13,630).[1][2][3][4][6]
other_requirements: Must be enrolled in Medicare Part A and/or B.
North Carolina resident.
U.S. citizen or qualified immigrant.
Not eligible for full Medicaid (MSPs are for Medicare beneficiaries with limited income/assets).

benefits.value: QMB: Medicare Part A premiums (if applicable), Part B premiums ($202.90/mo in 2026), deductibles, copays, coinsurance; providers prohibited from billing beneficiary for covered costs. SLMB: Part B premium only ($202.90/mo, ~$2,435/year). QI: Part B premium only ($202.90/mo, ~$2,435/year). All auto-qualify for full Extra Help (LIS) for Part D drugs, no separate application. Annual savings $2,400-$8,000+ depending on program.[1][2][4][5]

methods: Phone: Local county Department of Social Services (DSS) office; find via NC DHHS directory.
Online: NC Medicaid ePASS portal (epass.nc.gov).
Mail/In-person: Local county DSS office.
No specific national form; use state Medicaid application (Form DMA-3000 or equivalent via DSS).
application.processing_time: Typically 45 days, but varies by county; no specific statewide timeline stated.
application.waitlist: QI only: first-come, first-served with limited federal funding; may run out annually (reapply each year).[1][2][8]
forms: NC Medicaid Application (via ePASS or DMA-3000 at local DSS)
documents: Proof of income (SSI awards, pay stubs, pension statements for 1-2 months).
Proof of resources (bank statements, investment accounts).

geography.restrictions: Available statewide to NC Medicare beneficiaries; administered locally.
geography.regional_variations: Processing times and support vary by county DSS office; no benefit differences.
offices_or_providers: Local county Department of Social Services (DSS) offices across NC

gotchas: QI funding limited—first-come, first-served, may exhaust before year-end; reapply annually.[1][2][8]
Income includes $20 disregard; countable income must be under exact FPL-based limits—many miss eligibility due to unawareness.[2]
Assets include most liquid resources but exempt primary home/car; people overlook countable items like second vehicles.
Auto-triggers Extra Help for Part D, but confirm enrollment.
Providers cannot bill QMB for covered services, but some unaware—carry MSP card.
Outdated asset/income figures in older sources; always check current year.
Must have Medicare; not for full Medicaid eligibles.
programs.data_shape_notes: Tiered by income brackets (QMB lowest, QI highest); QI capped enrollment; asset limits fixed federally but income FPL-adjusted annually; local county administration with statewide uniformity in rules.

programs.name: NC Food and Nutrition Services (FNS)/SNAP

eligibility.income_limits: Households with a senior (60+) must have monthly net income at or below these thresholds after deductions (standard $193 for 1-4 members, 20% earnings deduction, medical expenses over $35/month for elderly/disabled): |Household Size|Maximum Monthly Net Income|Maximum Monthly Benefits Allotment| |1|$1,133|$281| |2|$1,526|$516| |3|$1,920|$740| |4|$2,313|$939| Thresholds increase with household size. For elderly/disabled-only households, only net income limit applies (no gross income test). Simplified SNAP variant for SSI recipients age 65+ has no specific income limit beyond SSI receipt.[1][3][5]
eligibility.asset_limits: For households with elderly (60+) or disabled members, countable resources must be $4,500 or less. Exemptions typically include primary home, one vehicle, household goods, retirement accounts, and certain life insurance; exact countable assets follow federal SNAP rules with state application.[5][6]
other_requirements: U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen (e.g., 5+ years residency, children under 18, disability benefits).
Reside in North Carolina.
For Simplified SNAP: Receive SSI, age 65+, purchase/prepare food separately, not in institution/ADTC, not on regular FNS elsewhere, not disqualified from FNS.[4][9]
Household includes those who buy/prepare food together.
Work requirements may apply (e.g., 80 hours/month work/volunteer/training for ages 55-64 without dependents under 2025 changes), but exemptions for elderly 60+.[5]

benefits.value: EBT card for purchasing food (groceries, fresh produce, non-alcoholic beverages at authorized retailers). Maximum monthly allotments as in income table (e.g., $281 for 1-person, actual amount based on income/deductions; average $188 for seniors). Simplified SNAP certified for 3 years.[1][4][5]

methods: Online: NC ePASS at https://epass.nc.gov
Phone: Local county DSS office (find via https://www.ncdhhs.gov/divisions/child-and-family-well-being/food-and-nutrition-services-food-stamps)
Mail/In-person: Local Department of Social Services (DSS) office by county
Automatic for Simplified SNAP via monthly NC State Data Exchange (SDX) match if eligible.
application.processing_time: 30 days standard; expedited within 7 days if income < $150 and cash < $100, or income/cash < rent/utilities.[2][4]
forms: Food and Nutrition Services Application (evaluated for Simplified SNAP automatically if eligible)
SNAP application mailed automatically for potential Simplified SNAP recipients.[4]
documents: Proof of income (Social Security, pension statements)
Proof of expenses (medical over $35/month, rent, utilities)
Bank statements for assets if applicable.

geography.restrictions: Must reside in North Carolina; apply via local county DSS.
geography.regional_variations: Administered uniformly by county DSS offices; potential processing/wait time differences by county workload, but no specified benefit variations.[4][6]
offices_or_providers: Local county Department of Social Services (DSS) offices statewide.

gotchas: Elderly households skip gross income test (only net matters), but must calculate deductions accurately (medical key for seniors).[1][3]
Simplified SNAP only for SSI/65+ separate food prep; married couples count as one household.[4]
Assets $4,500 limit applies to elderly/disabled (higher than non-elderly $3,000).[5]
Post-2025 changes: Work rules for 55-64, non-citizen restrictions; expedited SNAP often missed for urgent need.[2][8]
Include all who buy/prepare food in household; students 18-51 need exemptions.[7]
Benefits loaded on EBT card; can't buy hot foods/alcohol/tobacco.
programs.data_shape_notes: Net income only for elderly/disabled households (no gross test); deductions heavily favor seniors (medical, standard); Simplified SNAP auto-enrollment for SSI/65+; benefits scale by household size with max allotments; county DSS processing.

programs.name: NC Low-Income Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)

eligibility.age: 60 or older (priority tier); disabled persons receiving services through NC Division of Aging and Adult Services also qualify for priority tier
eligibility.income_limits: Priority households (age 60+ or disabled): up to 150% of Federal Poverty Level. All other households: up to 130% of Federal Poverty Level. For a household of four, the 130% threshold is $34,056 gross annual income ($2,838/month) as of the current funding cycle. Income limits vary by household size and may change year to year.[1][4]
eligibility.asset_limits: Countable resources (savings, checking accounts, cash on hand) must be at or below $2,250. One source indicates $3,000 for Union County specifically, suggesting possible regional variation.[1][6][7]
other_requirements: At least one U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen in the household must meet eligibility criteria[6]
Household must be responsible for paying the home heating/cooling bill[1]
Applicant must reside in the county where the application is submitted[7]
Applicant does not need to be behind on energy bills to qualify[3]

benefits.value: One-time vendor payment of $300, $400, or $500 sent directly to heating vendor. Last year (2023-2024), the program provided approximately $48 million to help more than 134,000 households.[2][3]

methods: Online: Visit NCDHHS website (specific URL not provided in search results; contact local social services department for link)
In-person: Local social services departments (funds distributed through these offices)[1]
Phone: Contact local county Department of Social Services or Division of Aging and Adult Services
application.processing_time: Not specified in search results; funds distributed on ongoing basis until exhausted
application.waitlist: No formal waitlist mentioned; program operates on first-come, first-served basis until funds are exhausted[1]
forms: Application form (specific form number not provided in search results)
documents: Proof of address: lease, rental agreement, or shelter expense documentation[7]
Citizenship/alien status verification via Immigration and Naturalization Service[7]
Valid Social Security card or number[7]
Earned income documentation: wage stubs, tax forms, or employer contact[7]
Unearned income documentation: benefit award letters, retirement statements[7]
Asset/resource documentation: bank statements, ownership records, court documents[7]

geography.restrictions: Applicant must reside in the county where the application is submitted[7]
geography.regional_variations: Asset limits may vary by county (most sources cite $2,250; Union County cites $3,000)[7]. Income eligibility thresholds are consistent statewide at 130% FPL for general households and 150% FPL for priority households, but dollar amounts vary by household size and may change year to year.[1][4]
geography.offices_or_providers: Local social services departments and county Departments of Social Services administer the program; no centralized office structure described

gotchas: Application windows are time-limited and critical: Priority households (age 60+ or disabled) can apply December 1-31 only; all other households can apply January 1-March 31 or until funds are exhausted. Missing these windows means waiting until the next funding cycle.[1][4][6][7]
North Carolina received approximately $96.3 million in LIHEAP funding for the current cycle, but $1.8 million is reserved for Tribal communities, reducing available funds for general population.[1]
Income limits are based on gross (pre-tax) income, not net income.[1]

Households that received LIEAP in the prior year (2023-2024) and meet priority criteria may receive automatic payments without reapplying, but only if they received notice of eligibility; those who did not receive notice must apply manually.[2]
The program provides only a one-time payment per funding cycle, not ongoing assistance.[1][2]
Payment goes directly to the heating vendor, not to the household, so applicants cannot redirect funds.[2]
Disabled persons must be receiving services through the NC Division of Aging and Adult Services to qualify for priority status; simply having a disability is insufficient.[1][4]
Asset limits are strict: even modest savings can disqualify a household if they exceed the threshold.[1]
programs.data_shape_notes: This program operates on a strict annual cycle with two application windows: a priority window (December 1-31) for elderly and disabled households, and a general window (January 1-March 31) for all other eligible households. Benefits are fixed at $300-$500 per household (not scaled by household size as one might expect) and vary only by primary heating source. The program is entirely dependent on annual federal appropriations and available funds; it is not an entitlement program. Income thresholds are percentage-based (130-150% of FPL) rather than fixed dollar amounts, meaning eligibility changes annually. County-level administration creates potential for minor regional variations in asset limits and processing procedures, though core eligibility criteria are statewide.

## Key Takeaways for Families

**Timing is everything:** If your elderly loved one is 60 or older, you have a narrow window—**December 1-31 each year**—to apply. Miss it, and you wait until January 1 of the next year when the general application window opens (January 1-March 31).[1][4]

**Income thresholds are generous for seniors:** Households with someone 60+ can earn up to **150% of the Federal Poverty Level**, compared to 130% for younger households. For a household of four, that's roughly $34,000-$39,000 annually, depending on the exact poverty threshold that year.[1][4]

**The payment is modest but direct:** You'll receive a **one-time payment of $300-$500** sent straight to your heating vendor, not to you. This is not ongoing assistance—it's a single annual benefit.[3]

**Funds run out:** North Carolina received $96.3 million for the current cycle, but that's distributed across the entire state. Once funds are exhausted, applications close, even if it's mid-March.[1]

**Prior recipients may get automatic payments:** If your household received LIEAP last year and meets priority criteria, you may receive an automatic payment without reapplying—but only if you received official notice. If you didn't get notice, you must apply manually.[2]

programs.name: NC Weatherization Assistance Program

eligibility.income_limits: Total household income at or below 200% of the federal poverty guidelines, or receiving Work First (TANF), Supplemental Security Income (SSI), or similar assistance. Example limits (likely 2024/2025 levels, subject to annual updates): 1 person: $31,300; 2: $42,300; 3: $53,300; 4: $64,300; 5: $75,300; 6: $86,300; 7: $97,300; 8: $108,300; add $11,000 per additional person.[2][5][6]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned in program guidelines.[1][2]
other_requirements: Household must reside in a home eligible for weatherization (not needing major repairs; single-family homes, duplexes, condos, mobile homes qualify).[2][3][4]
Homeowners or renters with written landlord permission.[1][2][4]
One service per household.[1]
Priority for elderly, disabled, or families with children when funds limited.[2][5][6]

benefits.value: In-home energy assessments and installations of energy-saving measures (e.g., insulation, air sealing, heating/cooling efficiency upgrades) at little to no cost. Average cost per household ~$2,500 in materials/labor; saves ~$215-$372/year on energy bills.[1][4][5][6]

methods: Contact NC Energy Office at 919-713-1570 for statewide info or local provider.[2]

Regional providers: e.g., Resources for Seniors (Wake/Durham: 919-713-1570, download packet); PTRC (Piedmont Triad: 336-904-0338 mailed app); EICCA (application request); Action Pathways (southeastern NC counties).[2][4][5][6]
Mail or in-person to local provider; online forms via some providers (e.g., EICCA link).[5]
Sign Customer Program Application and Assessment Form.[1]
application.processing_time: Not specified; first-come, first-served until funds exhausted. Two-step: income/home approval, then audit.[1][3]
application.waitlist: Funds limited; priority-based when funds low; no formal waitlist details.[1][2]
forms: Customer Program Application and Assessment Form; local provider applications (e.g., Resources for Seniors packet). [1][2]
documents: Proof of income for all household members (12 months).
Photo ID/driver's license (address matching service location).

geography.restrictions: Available in participating service territories (e.g., Dominion Energy NC); homes needing major repairs ineligible.[1][3]
geography.regional_variations: Administered by local agencies (e.g., Resources for Seniors in Wake/Durham; PTRC in Piedmont Triad; Action Pathways in 11 southeastern counties: Bladen, Brunswick, etc.; EICCA in its area). Contact info, forms, and processing vary by provider.[2][4][5][6]
offices_or_providers: NC DEQ Energy Office (statewide contact); local subgrantees per county/region.[2][8]

gotchas: Funds limited, first-come first-served; may exhaust quickly.[1]
Home must pass pre-assessment (not for major repairs/rehab).[2][3]
Renters need landlord permission; only one service per household.[1][4]
Income/SSI/TANF automatic qualifier, but priority for elderly/disabled/kids—still apply early.[2][6]
Verify current poverty guidelines (annual update); example table may be outdated.[2]
programs.data_shape_notes: Decentralized by region/local providers; DOE-funded statewide but contact county-specific agency; priority tiers; no age minimum but elderly prioritized; income at 200% FPL or SSI/TANF auto-qualify.

programs._raw:
I need to be direct with you: **the search results do not describe a single statewide "NC Home-Delivered Meals" program**. Instead, they reveal a fragmented system of **county-specific programs** operated by different local agencies, each with its own eligibility rules, application process, and service model.

This is the critical "gotcha" you need to understand before building your guide.

Here's what the data actually shows:

## The Reality: County-by-County, Not Statewide

The search results document home-delivered meals programs in at least six North Carolina counties—Pender, Haywood, Mecklenburg, Brunswick, Onslow, and Caldwell—each operated independently[1][2][3][4][8][9]. There is **no unified state program** with consistent eligibility, benefits, or application procedures across North Carolina.

## What IS Consistent Across Counties

**Eligibility (common across all documented programs):**
- **Age:** 60+ years old (though Brunswick County requires 65+)[1][2][3][4][8][9]
- **Homebound status:** Unable to leave home due to illness or disability[1][2][3][4][8][9]
- **Functional inability:** Cannot shop for or prepare meals due to physical or cognitive impairment[1][2][3][8][9]
- **No alternative support:** No family member, friend, or responsible person able and willing to help[1][3][8][9]
- **Residency:** Must live in the specific county where the program operates[1][2][3][4][8][9]

**What You Get (consistent across programs):**
- Nutritious meals delivered to the home, typically weekly[1][2][3][8][9]
- Short-term or long-term service options available[2]
- Meals can accommodate dietary restrictions[2][3]
- Spouses of eligible individuals may also qualify[2][3][9]

**Application Process (common pattern):**
- Phone screening assessment required[1]
- Home visit may be required to complete assessment[1]
- Referrals accepted from individuals, family members, medical providers, or caregivers[3]
- Waiting lists are typical[1][2]
- Periodic reassessment of continued eligibility[1]

## What Varies by County

| Aspect | Variation |
|--------|-----------|
| **Age requirement** | Most require 60+; Brunswick requires 65+[4] |
| **Application contact** | Pender: phone screening[1]; Mecklenburg: 704-432-1111 (Just1Call) or 704-336-3144[3]; Haywood: online application[2]; Onslow: county office[8]; Caldwell: BRCA[9] |
| **Income/asset limits** | Not specified in any search result |
| **Cost to recipient** | Pender, Onslow, Caldwell accept voluntary contributions but don't deny service if unable to pay[1][8][9]; others not specified |
| **Processing timeline** | Not specified in any search result |
| **Waitlist status** | All mention waiting lists exist; specific wait times not provided[1][2] |

## Critical Gaps in Available Information

The search results **do not provide**:
- Specific income or asset limits for any county program
- Exact processing timelines or current waitlist lengths
- Specific meal counts, caloric content, or service frequency (beyond "weekly")
- Formal application forms or form numbers
- Complete contact information for all counties
- Whether programs operate statewide or only in specific regions within each county
- Pricing or subsidy structures (beyond voluntary contributions)

## How to Build Your Guide

You have two options:

1. **County-by-county approach:** Create separate sections for each county's program, with contact information and specific eligibility rules. You'll need to contact each county's Area Agency on Aging or social services department directly to fill the gaps.

2. **General framework approach:** Provide the common eligibility criteria and application pattern, then direct families to contact their specific county's program for exact details. This is more honest given the fragmented nature of the system.

## The Gotchas

- **No statewide program exists:** Families cannot apply once and receive service across North Carolina.
- **Age cutoff varies:** Brunswick County's 65+ requirement differs from the 60+ standard elsewhere.
- **Waiting lists are universal:** Enrollment delays are expected in all documented programs.
- **County residency is strict:** Living just outside a county boundary may disqualify someone entirely.
- **No income test mentioned:** Unlike many social programs, these don't appear to have income limits—but this should be verified with each county.
- **Spouse eligibility is conditional:** Spouses qualify only if the primary applicant is eligible; they don't qualify independently.

Circuit Breaker program (separate) has tiered limits: $0-$38,800 (4% tax cap), $38,801-$55,050 (5% tax cap), over $55,050 ineligible, requires 5 years occupancy.

For disability: Certificate from NC-licensed physician or government agency
For veterans: Form AV-9 for VA certification

Proof of NC residency and property ownership/occupancy

Income based on prior full calendar year; exceeds limit = ineligible

Cannot receive other property tax relief simultaneously

programs.name: North Carolina Long-Term Care Medicaid

eligibility.income_limits: No fixed dollar limit; income must be less than the regional Medicaid nursing home pay rate (estimated $7,898–$11,218/month for singles in 2026, varying by facility/area). Nearly all income counts (IRA, pensions, Social Security, wages, etc.). Applicant pays most income toward care minus $70/month personal needs allowance, health premiums, and certain spousal allowances. For ABD Medicaid (basic coverage), single limit is $1,330/month (April 2026–March 2027). Married couples: spousal income generally not counted unless both applying; community spouse can retain up to half of joint assets up to $162,660 (2026).[1][2][3][6][7][10]
eligibility.asset_limits: Singles: $2,000 max in countable assets (cash, bank accounts, stocks, non-exempt property). Married (both needing care): $3,000. Exempt: primary home (equity up to $730,000 if intent to return, spouse/child under 21/disabled child lives there), one vehicle, personal belongings, life insurance (face value ≤$1,500), burial plots/funds.[2][3][7]
other_requirements: NC resident and US citizen/qualified immigrant.

Nursing Facility Level of Care (NFLOC): need full-time care based on assessment of Activities of Daily Living (ADLs: mobility, bathing, dressing, eating, toileting) and Instrumental ADLs (IADLs: cleaning, cooking, shopping, managing meds/bills), plus cognitive/behavioral issues.
Need care for 30+ consecutive days.

benefits.value: Coverage for nursing home care (full cost minus patient liability), assisted living, home/community-based services (HCBS) waivers (e.g., personal care, adult day health, home modifications), intermediate care facilities. Patient pays income toward facility cost (minus $70 PNA); Medicaid covers remainder. Specific HCBS varies by waiver/program (e.g., CAP/DA for disabled adults 18+, PACE for 55+ in select areas).[1][3][4][9]

methods: Online: epass.nc.gov (NC ePASS portal).
Phone: Local Department of Social Services (DSS) or NC Medicaid Contact Center at 1-888-245-0179.
In-person/mail: Local county DSS office (find via ncdhhs.gov/divisions/social-services/local-dss-directory).
Assisted: Through aging/cognitive supports specialists.
application.processing_time: 45–90 days for full determination, including NFLOC assessment.
application.waitlist: Yes, for HCBS waivers (e.g., CAP/DA); nursing home Medicaid generally no waitlist but bed availability varies.
forms: NC Medicaid Application (via ePASS or Form DSS-8209 for LTC), NFLOC assessment form (PREADMIT or PASRR).
documents: Income proof (bank statements, pay stubs, pension/SS awards last 3–6 months).
Asset statements (bank, investment, property deeds, vehicle titles).

geography.restrictions: HCBS waivers/PACE limited to specific service areas/counties.
geography.regional_variations: Nursing home pay rates/income thresholds vary by facility/area cost ($8,004–$11,093/month in 2025 estimates); waitlists longer for HCBS in high-demand regions; local DSS offices handle apps with varying processing.
offices_or_providers: Local county DSS offices; PACE organizations in select areas (e.g., Wake County LTC office).

gotchas: No single income cap—must be <facility's Medicaid rate, but all income pays toward care (only $70 PNA kept).
5-year look-back penalty for asset transfers/gifts.
HCBS waivers have waitlists; nursing home preferred for faster access.
Home equity limit $730,000; intent to return must be realistic.
Spousal impoverishment rules complex—community spouse asset allowance up to $162,660.
ABD Medicaid (65+/blind/disabled) needed first for LTC add-ons.
Medically needy spend-down via medical expenses if over limit.
programs.data_shape_notes: Income test is facility-specific pay rate (regional variation), not fixed amount; requires NFLOC assessment; multiple programs/waivers (nursing home vs HCBS) with tiered access/waitlists; spousal protections scale by joint assets.

programs.name: Project C.A.R.E. (Caregiver Alternatives to Running on Empty)

eligibility.income_limits: No specific income limits. Program gives priority to low-income individuals, minorities, and those in rural areas.[2][3]
other_requirements: Care recipient must have a diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease or related dementia (including Lewy Body Dementia, Vascular Dementia, Frontotemporal Dementia).[2]
Adult caregiver must recognize they can benefit from care consultation.[2][4]
Caregiver must be unpaid (family member or friend).[7]
Caregiver must be a North Carolina resident.[7]
Caregivers of persons currently receiving NC Medicaid CAP/DA Waiver are NOT eligible for care consultation, though they may receive dementia education, training, and referrals.[2]
Caregivers of persons on a waitlist for CAP/DA enrollment CAN be served.[2]
No age requirements for care recipients or caregivers.[2]

benefits.value: Care consultation; dementia-specific information and training; caregiver education; funds for self-directed respite care (subject to eligibility and availability); connections to community-based services and resources; referrals to Family Caregiver Support Program and Area Agencies on Aging.[2][3][6]
benefits.varies_by: Availability and individual need. Respite care vouchers are limited.[5] The level of assistance provided varies based on what the caregiver needs and prefers.[3]

methods: Phone: Contact your regional Area Agency on Aging or Just1Call intake line (example: 704-432-1111 for Mecklenburg County).[6]
In-person: Visit your regional Family Consultant or Area Agency on Aging office.[3]
Referral: Health and human service professionals can complete a referral application on behalf of a caregiver.[7]
Online eligibility pre-check available through some regional providers.[7]
application.waitlist: Not specified in available sources. Services can be provided while on waitlists for other programs like Medicaid CAP/DA.[3]
forms: NC DAAS FY2025 Project C.A.R.E. Host Agency Application (for organizations applying to be host agencies, not individual caregivers).[1]
Eligibility pre-check form (varies by regional provider).[7]
documents: Documentation of care recipient's dementia diagnosis (specific documents not detailed in sources).
Caregiver identification and relationship to care recipient (specific documents not detailed in sources).

geography.restrictions: Program operates through regional host agencies. Not all regions of North Carolina are currently served.[2]
geography.regional_variations: Services are provided through specific regional Area Agencies on Aging. Northeastern regions documented include: Region L (Edgecombe, Halifax, Nash, Northampton, Wilson); Region P (Carteret, Craven, Duplin, Greene, Jones, Lenoir, Onslow, Pamlico, Wayne); Region Q (Beaufort, Bertie, Herford, Martin, Pitt); Region R (Camden, Chowan, Currituck, Dare, Gates, Hyde, Pasquotank, Perquimans, Tyrrell, Washington).[2] Other regions have different host agencies (e.g., Land of Sky Area Agency on Aging, Cape Fear COG, Mecklenburg County).[4][5][6]
offices_or_providers: Land of Sky Area Agency on Aging (339 New Leicester Hwy, Suite 140).[4]
Albemarle Commission Area Agency on Aging (serves Regions L, P, Q, R).[2]
Western Piedmont Council of Governments (WPCOG).[3]
Cape Fear Council of Governments.[5]
Mecklenburg County (Just1Call: 704-432-1111).[6]
High Country Aging (828-265-5434 ext. 139).[7]

gotchas: Caregivers of persons CURRENTLY on Medicaid CAP/DA Waiver are ineligible for care consultation services, though they can still receive dementia education and referrals. This is a common point of confusion.[2]
There is NO income limit, but the program prioritizes low-income, minority, and rural families—meaning higher-income families may face longer waits or lower priority.[2][3]
Respite care vouchers are explicitly limited in availability.[5] Not all eligible caregivers will receive them.
The program is NOT statewide—coverage depends on which regional host agency serves your county. You must contact your specific regional provider.[2]
Processing time and waitlist status are not publicly documented, making it difficult to plan ahead.
The program began in 2001 and is described as the 'only state-funded, dementia-specific support' for caregivers in North Carolina, but availability varies significantly by region.[5]
programs.data_shape_notes: Project C.A.R.E. is a regionally fragmented program with no statewide uniform application process. Benefits are service-based rather than cash-based, with respite care vouchers being the primary financial component (limited availability). Eligibility is straightforward (dementia diagnosis + caregiver recognition of need) with no income or asset tests, but priority is given to underserved populations. The program's structure relies on regional Area Agencies on Aging as host agencies, meaning application methods, contact information, and processing times vary by county/region. No public data on processing times or waitlists is available.

## Key Takeaways for Families

**Before you apply, verify your region is served.** Project C.A.R.E. operates through regional host agencies, not uniformly statewide.[2] Contact your Area Agency on Aging to confirm coverage in your county.

**The main eligibility hurdle is the Medicaid CAP/DA Waiver rule.** If your loved one is already receiving this waiver, you cannot access care consultation—though dementia education and referrals are still available.[2] If they're on the waitlist for the waiver, you can be served now.

**Income doesn't disqualify you, but it affects priority.** There are no income limits, but the program prioritizes low-income, minority, and rural families.[2][3] Higher-income families may experience longer waits.

**Respite care vouchers are the most valuable benefit but are limited.** While the program offers education and consultation at no apparent cost, the respite care vouchers (funds for in-home or group respite care) are explicitly limited in availability.[5]

**Contact your regional provider directly.** Since there is no centralized online application portal documented in these sources, you'll need to call your regional Area Agency on Aging or use a referral from a health professional.[3][7] Processing times and current waitlist status are not publicly available.

programs.name: North Carolina Seniors Farmers' Market Nutrition Program
State-County Special Assistance - In Home

eligibility.age: 65 or older, or 18-64 and disabled per Social Security definition, or under 18 and legally blind[1][2][3]
eligibility.income_limits: Must be below federal poverty level or meet State/County Special Assistance income requirements (exact dollar amounts not specified in sources; same as traditional Special Assistance; higher for Enhanced Rate with Alzheimer's/dementia diagnosis)[1][2][5]
eligibility.asset_limits: Must meet State/County Special Assistance resource requirements (specific limits and exemptions not detailed in sources)[2][3]
other_requirements: U.S. citizen or qualified alien[1][2][3]
North Carolina resident[1][2][3]
Require level of care provided in licensed Adult Care Home (verified by doctor via FL-2 form) but desire to live at home[1][2][5]
Health, safety, and well-being can be maintained at home with services like in-home aide, home modifications, case management, supplies[1]
Receive SSI or financially ineligible for SSI solely due to excess income[3]
Not inmate of public institution[3]
All Medicaid and community resources considered first[1]

benefits.value: Monthly cash payments for living expenses (food, shelter, clothing, daily necessities); 2024 maximum rates: Basic $1,326/month, Enhanced $1,700/month (requires Alzheimer's/dementia diagnosis verified by FL-2); automatic Medicaid eligibility[2][5]

methods: Contact local county Department of Social Services (DSS)[2][5]
Wake County example: Call Catherine Goldman at 919-250-3835 for info or Tracy Gregory at 919-212-7549 to apply[1]
application.processing_time: Up to 45 days for 65+; up to 60 days for 18-64[1][2]
application.waitlist: Eliminated; eligible applicants approved without delay[5]
forms: FL-2 form completed by doctor to verify Adult Care Home level of care[2][5]
documents: Information on life and financial situation for assessment[1][2]

geography.restrictions: Available through county DSS offices statewide[2][5]
geography.regional_variations: Administered by local county DSS; contacts and processes may vary (e.g., specific phone numbers in Wake County); processing times standardized but potential regional differences in wait times or providers not detailed[1][2]
offices_or_providers: Local county Department of Social Services (DSS)

gotchas: Must exhaust Medicaid/community resources first[1]
Requires doctor-verified FL-2 form for Adult Care Home level of care[2][5]
Enhanced rate only for Alzheimer's/dementia with valid FL-2[2]
Case management mandatory: monthly contact, quarterly visits, annual assessment[1]
Spouse's income/assets do not affect eligibility[5]
Eligibility case-by-case; not automatic even if criteria met[1]
programs.data_shape_notes: County-administered statewide with local DSS variation; tiered benefits (Basic/Enhanced) by dementia diagnosis; no waitlist; scales by diagnosed needs rather than household size
